newLocations.name: Congo Basin Forest
newLocations.summary: The world’s second-largest rainforest, where rivers braid through cathedral forests and forest elephants open hidden trails.
newLocations.subtitle: Meet gorillas, Congo peafowl, colobus, and hornbills in a vast equatorial green heart.
habitat: River roads
Waterways are highways for animals and seeds across an otherwise continuous canopy.
Elephant engineers
Forest elephants create trails and clearings that other species use.
Canopy markets
Fruiting trees draw primates, hornbills, and insects into brief feeding gatherings.
Fieldcraft
Dawn choruses and dung piles often reveal more than a quick glance.

species.hook: Gorillas spend long days foraging in family groups, mostly on plants.
species.look: Dark bulky ape, silverback males, and carefully built ground nests.

quiz.q: What do gorillas mainly eat?

species.hook: Africa’s rainforest peafowl flashes green and bronze in deep shade.
species.look: Dark green-blue plumage, bare red throat skin on males, and shy understory habits.

quiz.q: Where does the Congo peafowl live?
quiz.explanation: It is a Central African forest endemic.

species.hook: Long black-and-white coats stream behind colobus as they leap between crowns.
species.look: Black body, white mantle and tail plume, and spectacular leaping locomotion.

quiz.q: What food specializes this monkey’s gut?

species.hook: Grey parrots are famous problem-solvers with complex social calls in the canopy.
species.look: Ash-gray body, bright red tail, and pale face mask.

quiz.q: What are African greys especially known for?

species.hook: A huge blue-green turaco bounds through crowns, balancing with a long tail.
species.look: Blue-green plumage, tall crest, yellow bill, and heavy canopy hops.

quiz.q: What do great blue turacos mainly seek?

species.hook: Forest elephants are master trail-makers of the Congo Basin.
species.look: Compact elephant body, straighter tusks, and dense-forest path networks.

quiz.q: Why are forest elephants called engineers?
options: They create trails and disperse seeds

species.hook: A rich brown back hides this kingfisher until it darts after understory prey.
species.look: Chocolate upperparts, bright blue rump, and a red bill.

quiz.q: Where does this kingfisher often hunt?
quiz.explanation: It is a forest interior hunter.

newLocations.name: Western Ghats
newLocations.summary: An ancient monsoon mountain chain where shola forests, grasslands, and streams hold extraordinary endemism.
newLocations.subtitle: Find lion-tailed macaques, Malabar trogons, frogmouths, and Nilgiri tahrs across wet slopes.
habitat: Monsoon engines
Seasonal rains recharge streams that carve biodiversity-rich valleys.
Shola mosaics
Patches of forest in grassland create edge habitats for specialists.
Endemic pressure
Many species exist nowhere else, so habitat continuity matters intensely.
Fieldcraft
Misty mornings reward patient scanning of canopy and cliffs.

species.hook: A silver mane frames this macaque’s black face like a lion’s ruff in the treetops.
species.look: Black body, gray mane, tufted tail tip, and arboreal habits.

quiz.q: Where is this macaque endemic?
quiz.explanation: It is a southern Indian endemic.

species.hook: A crimson-and-black trogon sits motionless, then flutters out like a sudden painted leaf.
species.look: Male with black head, crimson underparts, and finely vermiculated wings.

quiz.q: How do trogons often hunt?

species.hook: By day a frogmouth becomes bark and dead leaves; by night it becomes a silent insect trap.
species.look: Wide frog-like bill, mottled brown plumage, and daytime freeze posture.

quiz.q: When does the frogmouth usually feed?

species.hook: Stocky mountain ungulates cling to grassy cliffs above the clouds.
species.look: Brown goat-antelope form, short horns, and cliff-edge confidence.

quiz.q: What terrain do Nilgiri tahrs favor?

species.hook: Whooshing wingbeats announce a great hornbill long before its huge casque appears.
species.look: Black-and-yellow bill with casque, black-and-white wings, and loud whooshing flight.

quiz.q: Why do hornbills need old trees?

species.hook: Webbed feet turn leaps into glides between monsoon-forest trees.
species.look: Bright green body, extensive webbing, and tree-foam nests.

quiz.q: What does the webbing help this frog do?

species.hook: A dusky blue flycatcher of shola edges, snapping insects from misty perches.
species.look: Indigo-blue tones, pale undertail, and upright flycatcher posture.

quiz.q: Where is this flycatcher endemic?

newLocations.name: Atlantic Forest
newLocations.summary: A coastal rainforest once stretching for thousands of kilometers, now a mosaic of fragments still packed with endemics.
newLocations.subtitle: Spot toucanets, muriquis, golden lion tamarins, and colorful tanagers in remnant green corridors.
habitat: Fragment life
Wildlife persists in patches connected by corridors and regenerating forest.
Coastal mist
Ocean moisture feeds lush slopes and epiphyte gardens.
Endemic richness
Many birds and primates evolved here and nowhere else.
Fieldcraft
Canopy flocks move fast—watch mixed tanager parties.

species.hook: One of the world’s largest New World monkeys swings through Atlantic Forest crowns.
species.look: Long-limbed pale monkey with a prehensile tip to the tail.

quiz.q: What do muriquis need most across fragments?

species.hook: Electric color in a tiny package—tanagers often travel in glittering mixed flocks.
species.look: Blue head, red neck, green back, and active flocking behavior.

quiz.q: How do these tanagers often travel?

species.hook: An oversized bill becomes a precision fruit tool in coastal forest canopies.
species.look: Black body, yellow bib, and a large colorful bill.

quiz.q: What is the bill especially useful for?

species.hook: On tall legs like stilts, the maned wolf patrols forest edges and nearby grasslands.
species.look: Fox-red coat, long black legs, and erect mane along the back.

quiz.q: What food is surprisingly important to maned wolves?
options: Fruit as well as small prey

species.hook: A rare Atlantic Forest woodpecker chisels into large dead trunks for beetle larvae.
species.look: Cinnamon-rufous tones, pale bill, and a contrasting dark crown.

quiz.q: Why is deadwood important here?
options: It holds woodpecker food and nest sites
quiz.explanation: Larvae and cavities depend on deadwood.

species.hook: Sunrise-orange tamarins bound through low canopy like living sparks.
species.look: Brilliant golden fur, lion-like mane, and tiny agile body.

quiz.q: What social trait helps raise young?

species.hook: Its call can sound like a hammered anvil ringing through humid forest.
species.look: White male with bare turquoise throat skin, and a startling call.

quiz.q: Why is a loud call useful in dense forest?
options: It carries farther than visual signals
quiz.explanation: Sound travels through leaves and mist.

newLocations.name: Madagascar Dry Forest
newLocations.summary: An island evolutionary laboratory where baobabs, lemurs, and bizarre birds share thorny dry forests.
newLocations.subtitle: Discover sifakas, vangas, chameleons, and paradise flycatchers across habitats found nowhere else.
habitat: Isolation
Millions of years apart from continents produced lineages found nowhere else.
Spiny defense
Thorny plants store water and deter browsers in long dry seasons.
Lemur networks
Primates are primary seed movers across Madagascar’s forests.
Fieldcraft
Look for leaped landing marks and listen for vanga calls.

species.hook: Sifakas side-skip across open ground with ballet-like bipedal bounds.
species.look: White-and-maroon coat, long legs, and upright leaping locomotion.

quiz.q: How do sifakas often cross open ground?

species.hook: A sickle bill slips under bark like a living crowbar after hidden insects.
species.look: Black-and-white plumage and an extraordinarily curved pale bill.

quiz.q: What is the sickle bill for?

species.hook: Color shifts signal mood while a tongue strikes insects in milliseconds.
species.look: Laterally compressed body, turret eyes, and dramatic male color patterns.

quiz.q: How do chameleons catch prey?
quiz.explanation: The tongue projects at high speed.

species.hook: Males stream twin tail ribbons while snatching insects from sunlit gaps.
species.look: Long tail streamers on males and active flycatching from perches.

quiz.q: What do paradise flycatchers mainly hunt?

species.hook: Striped tails rise like question marks as troops sunbathe and forage together.
species.look: Gray body, black-and-white ringed tail, and highly social troops.

quiz.q: What is a classic ring-tailed lemur signal?
options: Raised striped tails and scent marks
quiz.explanation: Visual and scent signals organize troops.

species.hook: Star-like shell patterns camouflage this tortoise among spiny dry-forest litter.
species.look: High-domed shell with yellow radiating lines on dark scutes.

quiz.q: Why is this tortoise endangered?
quiz.explanation: Exploitation and habitat loss are key threats.

species.hook: A peculiar Madagascan endemic rolls and calls above the canopy in display.
species.look: Ash-gray male tones, cryptic females, and distinctive rolling display flight.

quiz.q: What makes the cuckoo-roller evolutionarily special?
options: It is a unique endemic lineage
It is a type of whale
quiz.explanation: It represents an ancient Madagascan radiation.

newLocations.name: Hawaiian Wet Forests
newLocations.summary: Volcanic slopes wrapped in misty forest where honeycreepers and native forest birds tell an isolation story.
newLocations.subtitle: Trace ʻapapane, ʻiʻiwi, nēnē, and other island specialists shaped by elevation and fragile food webs.
habitat: Volcano foundations
New land created empty niches that evolution filled with specialists.
Nectar networks
Native birds and ʻōhiʻa trees form tight pollination partnerships.
Invasion pressure
Introduced predators and disease make remaining native forests precious.
Fieldcraft
Listen for high thin honeycreeper calls in the canopy.

species.hook: Crimson ʻapapane bounce through flowering ʻōhiʻa like sparks in the mist.
species.look: Bright red body, black wings and tail, and a curved nectar bill.

quiz.q: What tree is central to ʻapapane feeding?

species.hook: A scarlet body and salmon bill make the ʻiʻiwi an emblem of Hawaiian forests.
species.look: Vivid red plumage, black wings, and a strongly decurved bill.

quiz.q: Why are higher elevations important for ʻiʻiwi?
options: Disease pressure is lower in cool forests
They nest only in ice caves

species.hook: The ʻio is Hawaiʻi’s only native hawk, soaring over forest and farmland edges.
species.look: Dark and light color morphs, broad buteo wings, and island endemism.

quiz.q: What is special about the Hawaiian hawk?
options: It is Hawaiʻi’s only native hawk
quiz.explanation: It is the islands’ endemic buteo.

species.hook: Hawaii’s state bird walks lava and scrub with less webbing than many geese.
species.look: Buff-cheeked goose with partially webbed feet and an upright walk.

quiz.q: What terrain shaped nēnē feet?
quiz.explanation: They walk more than they swim.

species.hook: A shy thrush-like songster whistles from misty understories on Hawaiʻi Island.
species.look: Gray-brown solitaire with a fluty song and retiring habits.

quiz.q: Why does the ʻōmaʻo matter for plants?

species.hook: Some wear a cartoon smile on their abdomen—nature’s most unexpected emoji.
species.look: Minute spider with highly variable face-like abdominal markings.

quiz.q: What is unusual about this spider?
quiz.explanation: Morph patterns can look like smiling faces.

species.hook: One of the world’s rarest seals hauls out on remote Hawaiian beaches.
species.look: Sleek brown seal with a small head and beach haul-outs.

quiz.q: Why is this seal famous in conservation?
options: It is critically rare and endemic to Hawaiʻi
It lives only in the Alps
quiz.explanation: It is an endangered Hawaiian endemic.

newLocations.name: Kakadu Wetlands
newLocations.summary: A monsoon floodplain where rock country meets paperbark swamps and spectacular waterbirds gather.
newLocations.subtitle: Look for magpie geese, storks, sea eagles, and bee-eaters as waters rise and fall.
habitat: Flood pulse
Summer rains refill floodplains; dry season concentrates life around remaining water.
Rock country
Ancient escarpments hold distinct plants and shelter.
Waterbird theater
Huge flocks track invertebrates and plants across wetlands.
Fieldcraft
Keep distance from water edges—crocodiles rule the shallows.

species.hook: Black-and-white geese graze wet grasslands in noisy, spectacular flocks.
species.look: Pied plumage, knobbed head in adults, and large wetland flocks.

quiz.q: When can feeding be especially productive?
options: As floodwaters fall and expose bulbs

species.hook: Australia’s jabiru stalks shallows with a huge bill and iridescent dark neck.
species.look: Black-and-white body, dark iridescent neck, and massive black bill.

quiz.q: What do black-necked storks mainly hunt?

species.hook: Estuarine crocodiles make every billabong edge a place for careful attention.
species.look: Armored giant with eyes at the waterline and immense power.

quiz.q: Why keep distance from Kakadu water edges?

species.hook: A palette of green, blue, and rufous dashes out to snatch bees midair.
species.look: Streamered tail, black eye line, and brilliant multicolor plumage.

quiz.q: How do bee-eaters often catch food?

species.hook: A pink comb and sprawling toes let this jacana stroll across lotus leaves.
species.look: Brown-and-white body, pink facial comb, and elongated toes.

quiz.q: What adaptation spreads their weight on leaves?

species.hook: Agile wallabies graze floodplain edges, bouncing into cover when alarmed.
species.look: Smallish wallaby, clean face markings, and quick hopping gait.

quiz.q: When are agile wallabies often active?

species.hook: A pale-headed eagle rules rivers and coasts with deep, deliberate wingbeats.
species.look: White head and underparts, gray upperwings, and huge stick nests.

quiz.q: What do these eagles mainly eat?

newLocations.name: Sundarbans Mangroves
newLocations.summary: The world’s largest mangrove forest, where tigers swim between tide-washed trees and rivers meet the sea.
newLocations.subtitle: Track mangrove specialists from tigers and fishing cats to kingfishers and rare finfoots.
habitat: Tidal breath
Rising water rearranges where animals can walk, swim, and hunt each day.
Salt architects
Mangrove roots trap sediment and build living coastlines.
Swimming tiger
A rare big cat adapted to moving through flooded forest.
Fieldcraft
Quiet boat edges reveal kingfishers and mudskipper kingdoms.

species.hook: In the Sundarbans, tigers swim between forest islands with remarkable ease.
species.look: Orange coat with black stripes, and a reputation for water confidence.

quiz.q: What makes Sundarbans tigers distinctive?
options: They regularly swim between forest patches
quiz.explanation: Aquatic travel is part of life here.

species.hook: A stocky wild cat hunts along mangrove creeks, taking fish and frogs with quick strikes.
species.look: Olive-gray spotted coat and shortish tail, often near water.

quiz.q: What habitat does the fishing cat favor?

species.hook: A huge orange bill flashes as this kingfisher drops on crabs in the mangroves.
species.look: Large orange bill, brown wings, and turquoise-orange contrast.

quiz.q: What prey is important for this kingfisher?

species.hook: Jewels of the mud: pittas hop among mangrove roots after snails and crabs.
species.look: Bright green, turquoise, and buff pitta colors with a stout bill.

quiz.q: Where does the mangrove pitta live?

species.hook: Large monitors patrol banks and swim channels with snake-like ease.
species.look: Long neck, dark banded body, and forked tongue sampling the air.

quiz.q: How do water monitors often travel between banks?

species.hook: A round-headed dolphin rises quietly in muddy channels near the mangrove coast.
species.look: Bulbous head, no prominent beak, and subtle surfacing in murky water.

quiz.q: What waters do Irrawaddy dolphins use?

species.hook: One of Asia’s most elusive waterbirds slips among mangrove roots like a mystery.
species.look: Skulking grebe-like habits, colorful bill, and lobed toes.

quiz.q: Why is this bird hard to see?
options: It is secretive in dense wetland cover
It is invisible to cameras always

newLocations.name: Danube Delta
newLocations.summary: Europe’s great reed wilderness, where the Danube frays into channels, lakes, and bird-rich marshes.
newLocations.subtitle: Meet pelicans, glossy ibises, white-tailed eagles, and reed-bed specialists in a living labyrinth.
habitat: Reed cities
Endless reeds hide nests, hunters, and secret channels.
Migration crossroads
Birds funnel through the delta between continents.
Fresh meets Black Sea
Brackish edges diversify fish and bird communities.
Fieldcraft
Dawn boat rides reveal pelicans lifting like pale aircraft.

species.hook: Pelicans may fish as a team, herding schools into shallows for easy scooping.
species.look: Massive white body, yellow pouch, and black flight feathers in broad wings.

quiz.q: How can pelicans improve fishing success?

species.hook: In good light their dark plumage turns bronze, green, and purple.
species.look: Curved bill, glossy iridescent dark plumage, and tight feeding groups.

quiz.q: What does the curved bill help with?

species.hook: A huge sea eagle patrols channels with plank-like wings and a white tail beacon.
species.look: Brown body, pale head with age, white tail, and enormous wingspan.

quiz.q: What is a clear adult field mark?

species.hook: Males sing from reed tops with black heads and white neck rings in breeding dress.
species.look: Breeding male black head and white collar; streaky brown otherwise.

quiz.q: Where do reed buntings typically nest?
options: In reed beds and wet vegetation

species.hook: A spatula bill sweeps side to side, snapping shut when it feels prey.
species.look: All-white plumage, black spoon bill with yellow tip, and long neck.

quiz.q: How does a spoonbill find food?
options: By sweeping its bill by touch
By echolocating like a bat only

species.hook: A fox-like canid with a raccoon mask snuffles through delta thickets.
species.look: Stocky canid, facial mask, short legs, and dense coat.

quiz.q: What best describes its diet?

species.hook: A small dark cormorant threads reed-fringed channels, diving after tiny fish.
species.look: Small all-dark cormorant with a short bill and reed-loving habits.

quiz.q: Where does this cormorant like to fish?

newLocations.name: Cerrado Savanna
newLocations.summary: A biodiversity powerhouse of wooded grasslands, where twisted fire-adapted trees meet anteaters and maned wolves.
newLocations.subtitle: Explore giant anteaters, seriemas, burrowing owls, and palm-nut specialists across fire-shaped savanna.
habitat: Fire ecology
Many plants resprout after fire; timing shapes the whole community.
Underground life
Deep roots and burrows store resilience through drought.
Termite towers
Mounds are apartments, lookouts, and hunting posts.
Fieldcraft
Early morning reveals anteaters before heat builds.

species.hook: A broom-like tail and tubular snout advertise the world’s most specialized anteater.
species.look: Long bushy tail, striped shoulder cape, and elongated snout.

quiz.q: What do giant anteaters mainly eat?

species.hook: Seriemas strut through grasslands like feathered sentries with loud yelping calls.
species.look: Crested head, long red legs, and upright walking posture.

quiz.q: How do seriemas often hunt?

species.hook: Long-legged owls nod from termite-country burrows in broad daylight.
species.look: Long legs, brown spotting, and a habit of standing on mounds.

quiz.q: Where do burrowing owls nest?

species.hook: A little streaked walker picks insects from short grass after rains.
species.look: Slender pipit shape, yellowish tones, and a bobbing walk.

quiz.q: Where does this pipit feed?
options: Only in deep rainforest canopy exclusively

species.hook: The largest armadillo digs like a living excavator through Cerrado soils.
species.look: Huge carapace, massive claws, and nocturnal fossorial habits.

quiz.q: Why are its burrows important to others?
options: They become shelters for many species

species.hook: Cobalt giants crack palm nuts that defeat almost every other beak.
species.look: Intense blue plumage and a massive arched bill with yellow skin accents.

quiz.q: What food shaped this macaw’s beak?

species.hook: Stilt legs help this canid see over tall Cerrado grass.
species.look: Long black legs, red coat, and dark dorsal mane.

quiz.q: What helps maned wolves see in tall grass?

newLocations.name: Los Llanos
newLocations.summary: A vast tropical floodplain savanna where seasons flip from dusty plains to inland seas of grass.
newLocations.subtitle: Watch scarlet ibises, anacondas, screamers, and grassland birds track the flood.
habitat: Seasonal sea of grass
Rains turn plains into wetlands; drought concentrates animals around water.
Ranch and wild
Wildlife shares a working landscape of cattle and wetlands.
Wading theater
Ibises, herons, and storks follow fish across shallow lagoons.
Fieldcraft
Golden hour reveals ibis flocks commuting to roosts.

species.hook: Intense scarlet flocks look like flying embers over Llanos lagoons.
species.look: Uniform bright scarlet plumage with black wingtips, and curved bills.

quiz.q: What colors these ibises so vividly?

species.hook: Towering storks patrol drying pools when prey concentrates.
species.look: White body, black naked neck, and a massive bill.

quiz.q: When can jabiru hunting be especially good?
options: When water falls and prey concentrates

species.hook: One of the world’s rarest crocodiles still patrols remote Llanos rivers.
species.look: Slender-snouted crocodilian silhouette in muddy rivers.

quiz.q: Why is this crocodile a conservation priority?
options: It is a type of bird
quiz.explanation: It is a threatened river endemic.

species.hook: Noisy macaw pairs commute between gallery forest and fruiting trees.
species.look: Red body, yellow wing band, long tail, and loud calls.

quiz.q: Which tool helps a macaw climb and eat?
quiz.explanation: The beak grips and cracks food.

species.hook: A strange spike crowns the head of this loud wetland grazer.
species.look: Mostly black-and-white heavy body with a forward head horn.

quiz.q: What unusual feature does this bird have on its head?
quiz.explanation: It has a distinctive horn-like feathered spike.

species.hook: The world’s heaviest snake lurks in Llanos channels and lagoons.
species.look: Massive olive patterned coils and eyes set high for surface lurking.

quiz.q: Where do green anacondas often hunt?

species.hook: Deer move between gallery forest and open Llanos depending on floodwaters.
species.look: Classic white-tailed deer form with a flagging white underside of the tail.

quiz.q: What do predators watch closely in the Llanos?
options: Deer and other herbivores near water

newLocations.name: Raja Ampat Reefs
newLocations.summary: A global epicenter of coral diversity where reef walls drop into deep, clear Pacific water.
newLocations.subtitle: Meet manta rays, wobbegongs, reef fish rainbows, and sea turtles across island-studded seas.
habitat: Mega-diversity
More coral species pack these reefs than almost anywhere on Earth.
Island labyrinth
Channels between islands create currents that feed plankton eaters.
Cleaning stations
Fish and mantas visit reef cleaners in daily rituals.
Fieldcraft
Calm mornings reward snorkeling above shallow coral gardens.

species.hook: Mantas barrel-roll through plankton soup with wings like black kites under water.
species.look: Broad diamond silhouette, white belly patches, and cephalic fins.

quiz.q: What do reef mantas mainly eat?

species.hook: A carpet shark with a fringed face vanishes against coral rubble until prey comes close.
species.look: Flat patterned body and dermal tassels around the mouth.

quiz.q: How does a wobbegong usually hunt?
options: By camouflaged ambush on the reef

species.hook: Birds-of-paradise flash strange wing standards in display clearings on nearby islands.
species.look: Dark male with long wing standards raised in display.

quiz.q: What are male standardwings famous for?
quiz.explanation: They are birds-of-paradise with showy displays.

species.hook: Green turtles graze seagrass meadows among the reef islands.
species.look: Smooth carapace, rounded head, and calm surface breaths.

quiz.q: What do adult green turtles often eat?

species.hook: A slender filefish picks polyps with a pointed snout—living proof of reef intimacy.
species.look: Green body with orange spots and a tubular snout.

quiz.q: What does this filefish specialize on?

species.hook: Electric blue surgeonfish flash through coral canyons in busy schools.
species.look: Bright blue body, black palette pattern, and yellow tail.

quiz.q: What do these surgeonfish mainly graze?

species.hook: Large pigeons commute between islands carrying nutmeg-sized seeds in their guts.
species.look: Hefty arboreal pigeon with soft gray and green tones.

quiz.q: Why are imperial pigeons important to islands?

newLocations.name: Belize Barrier Reef
newLocations.summary: The longest barrier reef in the Western Hemisphere, wrapping turquoise shallows and mangrove nurseries.
newLocations.subtitle: Spot manatees, frigatebirds, nurse sharks, and rainbow parrotfish across reef-to-mangrove mosaics.
habitat: Nursery network
Mangroves, seagrass, and reefs exchange juvenile fish through life stages.
Blue hole fame
Offshore atolls and drop-offs add deep-water drama beside shallow gardens.
Bird towers
Frigatebirds and boobies nest on remote cayes.
Fieldcraft
Glass calm water for turtles and rays over sand patches.

species.hook: Manatees munch seagrass meadows like underwater lawnmowers.
species.look: Large gray body, paddle tail, and gentle surface nostrils.

quiz.q: What do manatees mainly eat?

species.hook: Males inflate a scarlet throat balloon while piracy keeps flocks fed.
species.look: Long angled wings, forked tail, and scarlet male gular pouch.

quiz.q: What feeding strategy are frigatebirds known for?

species.hook: Nurse sharks loaf under ledges by day and vacuum prey from sand at night.
species.look: Tawny brown shark with barbels by the mouth and a blunt head.

quiz.q: How do nurse sharks often feed?

species.hook: Parrotfish crunch algae and help keep reefs from being smothered.
species.look: Large colorful scarid with a beak-like dental plate.

quiz.q: Why are parrotfish important to reefs?
options: They graze algae that can overgrow coral

species.hook: Pink spoonbills sweep mangrove shallows beside the reef system.
species.look: Bright pink plumage and a spatulate bill.

quiz.q: How do spoonbills find food?
options: By sweeping the bill by touch

species.hook: American crocodiles use lagoons and mangrove channels along the reef coast.
species.look: Narrower snout than alligators and a coastal habitat preference.

quiz.q: Where do American crocodiles often live here?

species.hook: Queen triggerfish overturn urchins and crunch hard-shelled reef prey.
species.look: Blue-and-yellow patterns, trigger dorsal spine, and strong jaws.

quiz.q: What kind of prey suits their jaws?

newLocations.name: Red Sea Coral Coast
newLocations.summary: Clear, warm waters host reefs adapted to high salinity and sun, with dramatic desert meeting sea.
newLocations.subtitle: Find lionfish, anemonefish, Napoleon wrasse, and seabirds along a desert-edged coral coast.
habitat: Desert meets reef
Almost no runoff means unusually clear water and vivid coral views.
Endemic flavor
Red Sea reefs hold many species with restricted ranges.
Steep walls
Reefs plunge quickly, stacking habitats in a short swim.
Fieldcraft
Morning light is best for spotting pelagics off the wall.

species.hook: Fanlike fins advertise a venomous ambush predator among Red Sea corals.
species.look: Red-and-white bands with long pectoral fins.

quiz.q: What should divers remember about lionfish?

species.hook: Orange clownfish hide in stinging anemones that protect them from predators.
species.look: Orange body with two white bands and anemone homes.

quiz.q: What partnership defines clownfish life?

species.hook: A giant wrasse with a forehead bump patrols reefs and can eat crown-of-thorns starfish.
species.look: Huge green-blue wrasse with a bulbous forehead in adults.

quiz.q: Why are humphead wrasse ecologically helpful?

species.hook: A sleek gull endemic to the Red Sea region patrols coasts and islands.
species.look: Dark hood in breeding dress and distinctive white eye crescents.

quiz.q: Where is this gull especially characteristic?
quiz.explanation: It is a Red Sea regional endemic.

species.hook: Bold black-and-white banners school in shimmering towers above reefs.
species.look: Tall dorsal filament and pied butterflyfish pattern.

quiz.q: How do bannerfish often appear?

species.hook: Orange anthias sparkle in clouds along Red Sea walls, tracking plankton.
species.look: Bright orange females/males with showy fins in aggregations.

quiz.q: What do anthias clouds feed on?

species.hook: Spinner dolphins leap and twirl in air before returning to night feeding offshore.
species.look: Slender dolphin with a long beak and spinning aerial displays.

quiz.q: What behavior gives spinner dolphins their name?

newLocations.name: Namib Desert Coast
newLocations.summary: One of Earth’s oldest deserts, where fog from the cold Atlantic sustains dune life beside seal colonies.
newLocations.subtitle: Discover fog-basking beetles, oryx, flamingos in desert lagoons, and shorebirds on a stark coast.
habitat: Fog harvest
Many organisms drink condensed fog instead of waiting for rain.
Dune seas
Wind-built dunes create moving habitats with specialized survivors.
Cold current coast
The Benguela current feeds seals and seabirds beside hyper-arid land.
Fieldcraft
Dawn fog reveals tracks and beetle activity on dune faces.

species.hook: This beetle tip-stands on dunes so fog water runs down into its mouth.
species.look: Shiny dark beetle in a head-stand pose on dune crests.

quiz.q: How does this beetle get water?
options: By basking in fog that condenses on its body

species.hook: Spear-horned oryx move through gravel plains with extraordinary water efficiency.
species.look: Pale body, black-and-white face mask, and long straight horns.

quiz.q: What are gemsbok especially adapted to?

species.hook: Pink flocks paint desert lagoons fed by coastal and inland saline waters.
species.look: Long pink legs and neck with a bent filter bill.

quiz.q: Where do these flamingos often feed here?

species.hook: Huge seal colonies roar on the desert coast where cold waters are fish-rich.
species.look: External ears, dense fur, and crowded rocky/beach colonies.

quiz.q: Why can huge seal colonies live beside a desert?
options: Cold ocean currents provide rich food

species.hook: A pale lark runs dune faces, perfectly matched to Namib sand.
species.look: Sandy rufous lark that stays on dunes.

quiz.q: What habitat is this lark tied to?
quiz.explanation: It is a dune endemic specialist.

species.hook: Tiny desert adders sidewind across hot dunes and bury themselves with only eyes exposed.
species.look: Small flat-headed adder with eyes on top of the head.

quiz.q: How does this adder move on loose sand?
options: By swimming only in rivers far away

species.hook: A small tern nests on desert shores and hunts in nearby coastal waters.
species.look: Petite pale tern with a black cap in breeding dress.

quiz.q: Where does the Damara tern nest?

newLocations.name: Atacama Desert
newLocations.summary: The world’s driest non-polar desert, where rare blooms, high volcanoes, and coastal fog oases support surprising life.
newLocations.subtitle: Find vicuñas, Andean flamingos, desert lizards, and oasis birds between salt flats and fog zones.
habitat: Absolute dryness
Some weather stations record years without measurable rain.
Salar lakes
High saline lagoons host specialized flamingos and shorebirds.
Fog oases
Coastal camanchaca fog feeds unique plant communities.
Fieldcraft
Dawn at salars reveals flamingos before winds rise.

species.hook: Vicuñas graze harsh Andean slopes in family groups with exquisite wool.
species.look: Slender pale camelid with a long neck and elegant stance.

quiz.q: What elevation lifestyle are vicuñas built for?

species.hook: Yellow-legged flamingos gather on Atacama salt lakes under volcano silhouettes.
species.look: Pale pink flamingo with yellow legs and a black-and-yellow bill.

quiz.q: Where do Andean flamingos feed?

species.hook: Lava lizards sprint between rocks in fog-cooled coastal desert.
species.look: Spiny small lizard on dark coastal rocks.

quiz.q: When are these lizards often active?
options: Only at midnight in caves always

species.hook: A gray tyrant flycatcher hunts insects across barren puna and desert edges.
species.look: Upright gray flycatcher on open ground.

quiz.q: Where does this tyrant hunt?

species.hook: These green cushions look like moss but are woody plants centuries old.
species.look: Hard bright-green cushions on high rocky slopes.

quiz.q: What is surprising about llareta?
options: It can be extremely old and woody
It is a type of penguin

species.hook: Leaf-eared mice forage desert nights, becoming prey for owls and foxes.
species.look: Large ears, soft brown body, and nocturnal habits.

quiz.q: When do these mice usually forage?
options: Only at noon on open ice

species.hook: A rare coot builds massive stone nest mounds in high Andean lakes near desert margins.
species.look: Dark coot with a unique horn-like frontal structure.

quiz.q: What nest material can horned coots use?

newLocations.name: Alaska North Slope
newLocations.summary: A vast Arctic coastal plain where caribou, owls, and shorebirds race a short intense summer.
newLocations.subtitle: Follow caribou, snowy owls, tundra swans, and foxes across continuous daylight breeding grounds.
habitat: Midnight sun
Round-the-clock light compresses breeding into a frantic summer.
Pond mosaic
Ice-wedge polygons create countless wetlands for insects and birds.
Migration endpoints
Shorebirds arrive from as far as South America to nest.
Fieldcraft
Bring binoculars for distant caribou and owl perches on pingos.

species.hook: Caribou rivers of antlers still cross the North Slope in one of Earth’s great migrations.
species.look: Medium deer with crescent hooves and both sexes often antlered.

quiz.q: What makes North Slope caribou famous?

species.hook: Snowy owls hunt in continuous daylight when lemming numbers boom.
species.look: White plumage, yellow eyes, and feathered feet.

quiz.q: What prey often shapes breeding success?

species.hook: Tundra swans nest by ponds then fly to distant winter wetlands.
species.look: All-white swan with a black bill usually showing a yellow spot.

quiz.q: Where do tundra swans nest?

species.hook: Males inflate a chest sac and boom over tundra territories after epic flights.
species.look: Medium sandpiper with a sharp breast streaking cutoff.

quiz.q: What is dramatic about their annual life?

species.hook: Arctic foxes switch coats and raid nests during the burst of summer life.
species.look: Compact fox with short ears and seasonal white or brown fur.

quiz.q: Why is a white winter coat useful?

species.hook: Red-throated loons may nest on puddle-sized pools yet commute to the sea to fish.
species.look: Low profile on water and a rust throat in breeding dress.

quiz.q: Where may they nest while fishing at sea?

species.hook: Muskoxen form defensive circles, wearing some of the warmest fur on Earth.
species.look: Stocky bovine form with curved horns and long guard hairs.

quiz.q: How do muskoxen often face predators?

newLocations.name: Iceland Highlands & Coast
newLocations.summary: A volcanic island of moss, glaciers, and seabird cliffs where North Atlantic productivity meets raw geology.
newLocations.subtitle: Meet puffins, gyrfalcons, Arctic foxes, and whales around a land of fire and ice.
habitat: Volcanic soils
Moss and pioneer plants colonize lava before shrubs arrive.
Seabird cities
Sea cliffs host dense breeding colonies fed by cold seas.
Marine giants
Whales use rich waters around the island shelf.
Fieldcraft
Evening light on cliffs reveals puffin traffic to burrows.

species.hook: Puffins return to burrows with beakfuls of sand eels like colorful sea parrots.
species.look: Black-and-white auk with a bright triangular summer bill.

quiz.q: What do puffins catch for chicks?

species.hook: The world’s largest falcon hunts ptarmigan and seabirds across open Icelandic country.
species.look: Large falcon in white, gray, or dark morphs.

quiz.q: What makes gyrfalcons distinctive?
quiz.explanation: They are the largest falcon species.

species.hook: Arctic foxes were Iceland’s sole native land mammal before humans arrived.
species.look: Compact fox; Icelandic foxes may stay brownish longer than high-Arctic peers.

quiz.q: What is historically special about Iceland’s Arctic fox?
options: It was the only native land mammal
quiz.explanation: It was Iceland’s sole native land mammal.

species.hook: Fulmars glide cliff faces like miniature albatrosses of the north.
species.look: Tube-nosed, gull-like seabird with stiff wings.

quiz.q: What nest defense can fulmars use?

species.adaptation: Long pectoral fins and lunge feeding
species.hook: Humpbacks feed in Iceland’s cold seas before migrating to warmer breeding waters.
species.look: Long white-marked pectorals and acrobatic breaches.

quiz.q: Why do humpbacks visit Icelandic waters?
options: To feed in productive cold seas
To nest in lava tubes only

species.hook: Pink-footed geese breed in Iceland’s highlands then pour into wintering grounds abroad.
species.look: Compact gray goose with pink feet and a small bill.

quiz.q: Where do many pink-footed geese breed?
quiz.explanation: Iceland is a major breeding area.

species.hook: Ptarmigan turn white in winter and mottled in summer to match Icelandic ground.
species.look: Chicken-like grouse with feathered feet and seasonal plumage.

quiz.q: Why do ptarmigan change color?

newLocations.name: Southern Appalachians
newLocations.summary: Ancient wrinkled mountains holding some of North America’s richest temperate forests and salamander diversity.
newLocations.subtitle: Find black bears, cerulean warblers, salamanders, and fireflies in cove forests and balds.
habitat: Old mountains
Long erosion created complex slopes and sheltered coves.
Salamander capital
Cool moist forests support extraordinary amphibian diversity.
Migrant songbirds
Neotropical migrants breed in layered canopies.
Fieldcraft
After rain, scan logs and seeps for salamanders.

species.hook: Black bears fatten on berries, nuts, and insects before winter denning.
species.look: Black (sometimes brown) bear with a straight facial profile.

quiz.q: What best describes black bear diet?

species.hook: Sky-blue males sing from the tallest Appalachian hardwood canopies.
species.look: Small warbler; males are blue above with a breast band.

quiz.q: Where do cerulean warblers often forage when breeding?

species.hook: Colorful woodland salamanders breathe through moist skin in cool Appalachian forests.
species.look: Slender salamander with distinctive color forms by mountain range.

quiz.q: How do many lungless salamanders breathe?

species.hook: Turkeys flock through oak woods, scratching leaf litter for mast and insects.
species.look: Large dark gamebird with a fan display in males.

quiz.q: What autumn food is important for turkeys?

species.hook: Pale green luna moths look like glowing leaves with trailing tails.
species.look: Large green silk moth with long hindwing tails and eyespots.

quiz.q: Do adult luna moths typically feed?
options: No, adults usually do not feed

species.hook: Males flash blue and black in rhododendron understories after migrating from the Caribbean.
species.look: Male blue above, black face/throat, white belly; females duller greenish.

quiz.q: Where do many of these warblers spend winter?

species.hook: Reintroduced elk bugle again in Southern Appalachian valleys.
species.look: Large deer with shaggy necks and branching antlers on bulls.

quiz.q: Why are Appalachian elk notable today?
options: They were restored after local extinction

newLocations.name: Valdivian Temperate Rainforest
newLocations.summary: A cool, dripping rainforest of ancient trees, where Andean deer, woodpeckers, and endemic marsupials share mossy halls.
newLocations.subtitle: Seek Magellanic woodpeckers, pudú, chucao tapaculos, and alerce giants in evergreen gloom.
habitat: Evergreen wet forest
Oceanic rainfall supports dense mosses and ferns.
Ancient trees
Fitzroya and other giants can live for millennia.
Endemic voices
Tapaculos and huet-huets call from dense bamboo understories.
Fieldcraft
Stand still—chucao often approach curious hikers.

species.hook: South America’s largest woodpecker hammers like a carpenter in old Valdivian trunks.
species.look: Black woodpecker with a scarlet crest on males.

quiz.q: Why do they need large old trees?
options: For foraging and nesting in big trunks
quiz.explanation: Large snags and trunks are essential.

species.hook: One of the world’s smallest deer slips through bamboo thickets like a living toy.
species.look: Tiny brown deer with short spiky antlers on males.

quiz.q: What is notable about pudú size?
options: They are among the smallest deer
They are larger than moose always

species.hook: Chucaos shout from bamboo and often hop into view with fearless curiosity.
species.look: Rufous-breasted wren-like bird of dense understory.

quiz.q: Where do chucaos usually stay?

species.hook: A tiny “little mountain monkey” is a living link to ancient marsupial lines.
species.look: Small brown marsupial with dense fur and nocturnal habits.

quiz.q: Why is monito del monte scientifically special?
options: It represents an ancient marsupial lineage
It is a type of penguin

species.hook: Long-billed parakeets exploit seeds of monkey-puzzle and other southern trees.
species.look: Green Chilean parakeet with an elongated upper mandible.

quiz.q: What food matches their long bill?

species.hook: Alerce trees can live thousands of years in cold wet Andes foothills.
species.look: Towering conifer with thick fibrous bark and tiny scale leaves.

quiz.q: What is remarkable about alerce?

species.hook: Elegant swans with black necks grace lakes at the rainforest edge.
species.look: White body, black neck, and a red knob at the bill base.

quiz.q: What plants do these swans often eat?
options: Only pine bark far inland exclusively

newLocations.name: European Alps
newLocations.summary: A folded mountain world of meadows, cliffs, and glaciers where ibex, eagles, and alpine birds follow the snowline.
newLocations.subtitle: Spot ibex, chamois, golden eagles, wallcreepers, and marmots across vertical seasons.
habitat: Vertical zones
Climb from forest to meadow to rock and ice in a day.
Summer meadows
Snowmelt unlocks brief flower and insect bonanzas.
Cliff specialists
Some birds nest on walls that seem impossible.
Fieldcraft
Listen for marmot whistles warning of eagles.

species.hook: Ibex stroll cliffs where hikers use ropes, scraping lichen and herbs from ledges.
species.look: Brown goat-antelope with huge backward-curving horns on males.

quiz.q: What terrain do ibex master?

species.hook: Chamois bound across scree with hooked horns and perfect balance.
species.look: Goat-antelope with a dark face stripe and slender hooked horns.

quiz.q: How do chamois escape danger?
quiz.explanation: They are superb runners on slopes.

species.hook: Golden eagles ride alpine winds, scanning meadows for marmots.
species.look: Large dark eagle with golden nape wash and long broad wings.

quiz.q: What prey do alpine golden eagles often watch?

species.hook: A wallcreeper flutters like a butterfly across sheer cliffs, probing cracks for insects.
species.look: Gray bird that flashes crimson wings against rock walls.

quiz.q: Where does a wallcreeper forage?
options: Only on sandy beaches tropical exclusively

species.hook: Marmots whistle alarms across meadows then vanish into burrows before winter sleep.
species.look: Chunky burrowing rodent with a bushy tail and social colonies.

quiz.q: How do marmots survive alpine winters?

species.hook: Snowfinches forage at the edge of snowfields, flashing white wings in flight.
species.look: Sparrow-like alpine bird with bold white wing panels.

quiz.q: What elevation lifestyle do snowfinches prefer?

species.hook: Bearded vultures drop bones onto rocks to reach rich marrow.
species.look: Huge vulture with a wedge tail and rusty body wash.

quiz.q: What unusual food do they specialize on?
options: Only live fish at sea exclusively

newLocations.name: Komodo & Lesser Sunda Dry Forests
newLocations.summary: Hot, hilly islands where the world’s largest lizards hunt deer among monsoon dry forests and coral coasts.
newLocations.subtitle: Meet Komodo dragons, orange-footed scrubfowl, friarbirds, and coastal frigatebirds on a dry-island stage.
habitat: Island giants
Isolation and prey shaped a gigantic monitor lizard.
Dry forest
Deciduous woods and savanna patches cover volcanic hills.
Sea around stone
Coral coasts and mangroves wrap dragon islands.
Fieldcraft
Keep respectful distance—dragons can move faster than they look.

species.hook: The world’s largest lizard ambushes deer and scavenges with a forked tongue reading the air.
species.look: Massive monitor with a long tail and powerful limbs.

quiz.q: What makes Komodo dragons unique?
options: They are the largest living lizards

species.hook: Deer are key prey that shape where dragons hunt on the hills.
species.look: Medium russet deer of Indonesian islands.

quiz.q: Why do deer matter to Komodo ecology?

species.hook: Scrubfowl build huge incubation mounds and let heat hatch their eggs.
species.look: Brown megapode with bright orange legs and a small crest.

quiz.q: How do scrubfowl incubate eggs?
options: By carrying them in pouches only

species.hook: Bare-headed friarbirds shout from dry-forest edges and village trees.
species.look: Brown honeyeater-like bird with a knobbed bare head.

quiz.q: What foods do friarbirds use?

species.hook: A white cockatoo with a bright yellow crest is now rare after trapping and habitat loss.
species.look: White cockatoo with an erectile yellow crest.

quiz.q: Why is this cockatoo a conservation concern?

species.hook: Frigatebirds soar over island coasts, snatching fish and harassing other seabirds.
species.look: Long angled wings, forked tail, and mostly black plumage.

quiz.q: What feeding habit are frigatebirds known for?

species.hook: Feral buffalo graze Komodo’s valleys, shaping grass height and dragon encounter zones.
species.look: Large dark bovine with curved horns.

quiz.q: How do buffalo change the island?

newLocations.name: Camargue Wetlands
newLocations.summary: A Mediterranean delta of salt flats, reedbeds, and marshes famous for flamingos, bulls, and white horses.
newLocations.subtitle: Watch greater flamingos, pied avocets, marsh harriers, and stilts across saline lagoons.
habitat: Salt and fresh
A mosaic of salinity creates many bird niches in one delta.
Iconic herds
Horses and bulls are cultural neighbors to wild flocks.
Migration stage
European waterbirds funnel through Mediterranean wetlands.
Fieldcraft
Heat haze hides birds—use a scope on lagoon islands.

species.hook: The Camargue’s pink flocks are a symbol of Mediterranean wetlands.
species.look: Tall pink flamingo with a black-edged bent bill.

quiz.q: What habitat do these flamingos need?

species.hook: Avocets sweep scythe-shaped bills through water to catch tiny prey.
species.look: Black-and-white shorebird with an upcurved bill.

quiz.q: How does an avocet feed?

species.hook: Marsh harriers glide low over reeds, surprising prey in dense cover.
species.look: Long-winged raptor with a pale crown on many males.

quiz.q: Where do marsh harriers usually hunt?
options: Only on open lava deserts exclusively

species.hook: Stilts look like they are walking on red stilts across mirror-shallow water.
species.look: White-and-black shorebird with absurdly long red legs.

quiz.q: What is the most obvious field mark?

species.hook: A turquoise dart plunges from reed perches into Camargue canals.
species.look: Tiny brilliant blue-and-orange kingfisher.

quiz.q: How does this kingfisher catch fish?

species.hook: Returning beavers reshape European wetlands with dams and felled trees.
species.look: Large rodent with a flat tail and swimming prowess.

quiz.q: Why are beavers called engineers?
options: They dam water and fell trees

species.hook: Pratincoles hunt like terns and swallows over dry marsh edges.
species.look: Brown tern-like shorebird with a creamy throat bordered in black.

quiz.q: How do pratincoles often feed?
options: By catching insects in the air

amazon.hook: A chunky green kingfisher watches Amazon channels, then dives for fish.
amazon.look: Green upperparts, white collar, and a heavy bill.

quiz.q: How does this kingfisher catch prey?

amazon.hook: Capuchins crack nuts and use tools with remarkable problem-solving skill.
amazon.look: Brown monkey with a distinctive cap-like crown.

quiz.q: What are capuchins especially known for?
quiz.explanation: They are notable primate tool users.

amazon.hook: Scarlet flocks can appear like flying embers over flooded Amazon edges.
amazon.look: Uniform bright scarlet plumage with black wingtips.

quiz.q: What colors their feathers?

amazon.hook: The heaviest snake waits in Amazonian backwaters for unwary prey.
amazon.look: Massive olive coils and high-set eyes.

quiz.q: Where do anacondas often hunt?

serengeti.hook: Hyenas both hunt and scavenge, with whoops organizing clans.
serengeti.look: Spotted coat, sloping back, and powerful forequarters.

quiz.q: What are hyena jaws especially good at?

serengeti.hook: Africa’s heaviest flying bird strides through grass after insects and seeds.
serengeti.look: Large gray-brown bustard with a thick neck.

quiz.q: What is notable about kori bustards?
options: They are among the heaviest flying birds

serengeti.hook: Rollers flash every color while sallying from savanna perches.
serengeti.look: Turquoise wings, lilac breast, and long tail streamers.

quiz.q: How do rollers often catch prey?

serengeti.hook: Baboon troops sweep the plains, reading predator risk as a group.
serengeti.look: Large dog-faced monkey with a short olive-brown coat.

quiz.q: How do baboons often travel?

great-barrier-reef.hook: A thick moray waits in reef holes with only a watchful head exposed.
great-barrier-reef.look: Mottled heavy-bodied eel in coral crevices.

quiz.q: Where do morays usually hide?

great-barrier-reef.hook: This spiny starfish can devastate coral when populations explode.

quiz.q: What does it eat?

great-barrier-reef.hook: Pale terns hover and plunge in reef lagoons with elegant precision.
great-barrier-reef.look: White tern with a neat black nape line.

quiz.q: How do these terns catch fish?

great-barrier-reef.hook: Electric-blue stars crawl reef sand and rubble in plain sight.

quiz.q: Where is this sea star commonly seen?

svalbard.hook: Ptarmigan match snow and rock as seasons flip in the high Arctic.
svalbard.look: Feathered-foot grouse with seasonal white/brown plumage.

quiz.q: Why do ptarmigan molt color?

svalbard.hook: Kittiwakes glue nests to sheer cliffs and cry their own name over Arctic seas.
svalbard.look: Small gull with black wing tips and black legs.

quiz.q: Where do kittiwakes nest?

svalbard.hook: Ringed seals maintain breathing holes in sea ice and birth pups in snow lairs.
svalbard.look: Small seal with ring-like coat markings.

quiz.q: Why is sea ice important to ringed seals?
options: For breathing holes and pup lairs

svalbard.hook: Barnacle geese graze tundra then migrate to distant winter pastures.
svalbard.look: Black-and-white goose with a white face.

quiz.q: Where do barnacle geese breed?

galapagos.hook: Pink flamingos paint Galápagos saline lagoons against black lava.
galapagos.look: Tall pink flamingo with a bent filter bill.

quiz.q: What habitat do they use in Galápagos?

galapagos.hook: Nazca boobies nest on cliffs and plunge for fish in cool upwelling waters.
galapagos.look: White booby with a dark face mask and dagger bill.

quiz.q: How do boobies catch fish?

galapagos.hook: Fur seals hide in shady lava caves by day and feed in cool waters by night.
galapagos.look: Small fur seal with external ears and dense fur.

quiz.q: When do they often feed?

galapagos.hook: Colorful locusts are important prey for Galápagos hawks and other hunters.
galapagos.look: Large colorful grasshopper of arid islands.

quiz.q: Why do locusts matter here?
options: They are key prey in island food webs

everglades.hook: Limpkins wail at dusk and extract apple snails with a curved bill tip.
everglades.look: Brown streaked wading bird with a long slightly curved bill.

quiz.q: What is a limpkin’s key prey?

everglades.hook: Slender herons dash and freeze in Everglades shallows after tiny fish.
everglades.look: Blue-gray heron with a white belly stripe and long bill.

quiz.q: Where do tricolored herons hunt?

everglades.hook: Otters twist through canals, a sign of healthier freshwater connections.
everglades.look: Sleek brown mustelid with a tapered tail.

quiz.q: What do river otters mainly hunt?

everglades.hook: Scissor-tailed kites float over cypress strands snatching insects in flight.
everglades.look: Black-and-white raptor with a deeply forked tail.

quiz.q: What is a clear field mark?

yellowstone.hook: Ospreys hover over Yellowstone lakes then plunge feet-first for trout.
yellowstone.look: Dark eye stripe, white belly, and crooked wings in flight.

quiz.q: What do ospreys mainly eat?

yellowstone.hook: Moose wade willow flats and ponds, a northern giant in Rocky Mountain wetlands.
yellowstone.look: Huge deer with a shoulder hump and palmate antlers on bulls.

quiz.q: Where do moose often feed in summer?

yellowstone.hook: Pikas gather hay piles among talus to survive long winters without hibernating.
yellowstone.look: Small round-eared lagomorph in rocky slopes.

quiz.q: How do pikas prepare for winter?

yellowstone.hook: Cutthroat trout stitch together aquatic food webs from bugs to eagles and bears.
yellowstone.look: Trout with red-orange throat slash markings.

quiz.q: What eats cutthroat trout in Yellowstone?

sonoran.hook: Loud wrens build bulky nests in cholla and saguaro armor.
sonoran.look: Large streaked wren with a white eyebrow.

quiz.q: Where do cactus wrens often nest?

sonoran.hook: Bighorns bound rocky slopes where predators struggle to follow.
sonoran.look: Tan sheep with massive curled horns on rams.

quiz.q: What terrain helps bighorns escape?

sonoran.hook: Gilded flickers carve nest holes that later house owls, kestrels, and bats.
sonoran.look: Desert woodpecker with golden wing flashes and a brown-barred back.

quiz.q: Why are their nest holes important?

sonoran.hook: Kangaroo rats hop through dunes and can meet water needs from dry seeds.
sonoran.look: Long-tailed bipedal rodent with huge hind legs.

quiz.q: When do kangaroo rats usually forage?

monteverde.hook: A red-and-green trogon waits motionless, then flutters out through misty gaps.
monteverde.look: Green upperparts, red belly, and a neat white breast band.

quiz.q: How do trogons often hunt?

monteverde.hook: Active warblers glean insects in mixed flocks through cloud-forest understory.
monteverde.look: Yellowish warbler with three dark head stripes.

quiz.q: Where do they often forage?

monteverde.hook: Agoutis bury seeds and forget some—accidental tree planters of the cloud forest.
monteverde.look: Large diurnal rodent with a sleek reddish coat.

quiz.q: How do agoutis help trees?

pantanal.hook: Caimans pack Pantanal channels, basking by day and hunting in murky water.
pantanal.look: Medium crocodilian with a broad snout and dark patterning.

quiz.q: Where are yacare caimans abundant?

pantanal.hook: Blue-and-gold macaws scream across gallery forest, cracking hard seeds.
pantanal.look: Blue upperparts, yellow underparts, and a long tail.

quiz.q: What does their beak help them eat?

pantanal.hook: Wood storks sweep bills through murky Pantanal pools as waters fall.
pantanal.look: White body, dark head, and heavy bill.

quiz.q: When can hunting improve?
options: When water falls and prey concentrates

delaware-bay.hook: Sanderlings sprint after retreating waves like tiny clockwork toys.
delaware-bay.look: Pale sandpiper that chases waves on beaches.

quiz.q: How do sanderlings often feed?
quiz.explanation: They forage in the wave wash.

delaware-bay.hook: Bright bills pry open mussels and clams on bay shores.
delaware-bay.look: Black-and-white shorebird with a vivid orange bill.

quiz.q: What do oystercatchers eat?

delaware-bay.hook: Secretive sparrows sing from cordgrass where tides rule nesting success.

quiz.q: Where do seaside sparrows live?

okavango.hook: Buffalo herds reshape floodplain grass and stand their ground against lions.
okavango.look: Heavy bovine with fused horn bosses on many adults.

quiz.q: How do buffalo often reduce predation risk?

okavango.hook: Sacred ibises probe mud and edges across the delta’s drying pans.
okavango.look: White body, black head and plumes, and a curved bill.

quiz.q: What shape is their bill?
options: Straight like a spear only always

okavango.hook: A rare slate-colored egret hunts Okavango shallows with careful steps.
okavango.look: Dark gray egret with a yellowish bill and legs.

quiz.q: Where is the slaty egret especially associated?
quiz.explanation: It is a south-central African wetland bird.

fiordland.hook: The world’s only flightless parrot booms across island nights in managed sanctuaries.
fiordland.look: Large green mossy-looking parrot that cannot fly.

quiz.q: What is unique about kākāpō?
options: It is a flightless nocturnal parrot
quiz.explanation: It is a unique flightless parrot.

fiordland.hook: New Zealand’s tiny rifleman spirals up trunks gleaning insects like a living spark.

quiz.q: Where do riflemen often forage?

fiordland.hook: Fur seals pack Fiordland rocks, commuting into rich coastal waters.
fiordland.look: External ears, pointed snout, and rocky colonies.

quiz.q: Where do they rest on land?

borneo.hook: Pygmy elephants move quietly through Bornean floodplain forests.
borneo.look: Compact Asian elephant form of Borneo’s forests.

quiz.q: Why are elephants important in Borneo forests?
options: They open trails and disperse seeds

borneo.hook: Huge webbed feet let this frog glide between rainforest trees.
borneo.look: Bright green frog with extensive black-and-yellow webbing.

quiz.q: What do the webbed feet help it do?

borneo.hook: A rare hornbill with a solid casque needs immense old trees and intact forests.
borneo.look: Dark hornbill with an elongated central tail feather and heavy casque.

quiz.q: Why is this hornbill highly threatened?
options: Hunting for casques and habitat loss
quiz.explanation: Casque trade and forest loss hurt it.

celtic-cliffs.hook: Puffins ferry sand eels into cliff-top burrows through summer fog.
celtic-cliffs.look: Colorful-billed auk of North Atlantic cliffs.

quiz.q: What do puffins bring to chicks?

celtic-cliffs.hook: Shags dive sleek green-black bodies through kelp-filled inshore waters.
celtic-cliffs.look: Dark cormorant-like seabird with a thin bill and crest in breeding season.

quiz.q: Where do shags usually fish?
options: Only alpine lakes exclusively far inland

celtic-cliffs.hook: Harbor porpoises roll quietly in tidal races around Shetland shores.
celtic-cliffs.look: Small blunt-nosed porpoise with a triangular dorsal fin.

quiz.q: What waters do harbor porpoises favor?
options: Only rainforest rivers in the Amazon exclusively

himalaya.hook: Huge vultures ride mountain thermals to carcasses across Himalayan valleys.
himalaya.look: Very large pale vulture with a bare head and broad wings.

quiz.q: What do griffon vultures eat?
options: Only live fish at sea exclusively

himalaya.hook: Pikas whistle from Himalayan meadows, keystone prey for many predators.
himalaya.look: Small round-eared lagomorph of open highlands.

quiz.q: Why are plateau pikas ecologically important?
options: They are keystone prey and burrow engineers

himalaya.hook: One of the world’s rarest herons stalks remote Himalayan rivers.
himalaya.look: Huge dark heron with a white belly.

quiz.q: Why is this heron a conservation priority?
options: It is extremely rare and river-dependent
It is a type of seal
quiz.explanation: It is a threatened river specialist.
